input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: From a deontological perspective, the moral duty is to refrain from actively causing harm to the track workman. It is impermissible to intentionally divert the trolley to kill one person, even if it means saving five others. The moral rule here is that it is wrong to use a person as a means to an end, regardless of the consequences. The intention to kill is what makes the action morally wrong. Therefore, I would not turn the trolley.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but from a utilitarian standpoint, the goal is to minimize total harm. If we don't turn the trolley, five people will die. By turning it, only one person dies. The outcome with the least total harm is achieved by turning the trolley. The intention is to save lives, not to kill one person. The consequences matter more than the action itself.

[Deontologist]: I see your point, but I must disagree. The consequences do not justify the means. By turning the trolley, you are actively choosing to kill one person. That is a violation of the moral duty to respect the autonomy and right to life of every individual. The intention behind the action is crucial. Even if the outcome seems more favorable, the act of intentionally causing harm to one person is morally impermissible. It's not about the total harm minimized, but about the moral rules that should not be violated.

[Utilitarian]: But if we prioritize moral rules over consequences, we risk creating a system where suffering is allowed to persist if it doesn't violate a specific rule. In this scenario, five lives are at stake. Isn't it our moral responsibility to act in a way that saves the most lives, even if it means making a difficult decision? The intention is to save lives, not to kill one person. The trolley's path is already set to kill five; we're merely choosing the lesser of two evils.

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern about suffering, but deontological ethics maintains that certain actions are inherently wrong, regardless of the consequences. It's not about allowing suffering to persist, but about respecting the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. By actively choosing to kill one person, we are devaluing that individual's life and treating them as a mere means to an end. The moral responsibility is to uphold these principles, not to prioritize the greater good at the expense of individual rights. The intention to kill, even if it's to save others, is what makes the action morally wrong.

[Utilitarian]: I see where you're coming from, but let's consider a different angle. What if the one person on the spur track was a known murderer or a tyrant whose actions have caused immense suffering? Would it still be morally wrong to turn the trolley? Utilitarianism would argue that the value of the lives saved outweighs the life of one who has caused harm. The intention is still to save lives, and the consequences would still be the lesser of two evils.

[Deontologist]: Even if the person on the spur track is a known murderer or tyrant, the moral rule against actively causing harm still applies. The principle of justice and fairness demands that we do not take it upon ourselves to execute or punish individuals, regardless of their past actions. That is the role of a just legal system, not an individual's impulsive decision in a moment of crisis. The intention to kill remains morally impermissible, as it violates the duty to respect the inherent right to life of every individual. Furthermore, we must consider the potential consequences of setting a precedent where it is acceptable to sacrifice one life, even that of a wrongdoer, for the sake of the greater good. This could lead to a slippery slope where the lives of the vulnerable are increasingly devalued.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your perspective, but let's consider a different scenario. What if the five people on the main track were not just any five people, but five renowned scientists working on a cure for a deadly pandemic that threatens millions of lives? Would it still be morally wrong to turn the trolley? The potential benefit to humanity is immense, and the intention is to save countless lives, not just the five on the track.

[Deontologist]: Even in this scenario, the moral rule against actively causing harm to an innocent individual remains paramount. The potential benefit to humanity does not justify the intentional taking of a life. It is crucial to uphold the principle that the ends do not justify the means. Furthermore, we must consider the potential consequences of making such a decision. If we allow exceptions to be made based on the perceived greater good, we risk undermining the very principles that protect individual rights and the sanctity of life. The intention to kill, even with the noblest of goals in mind, is still morally impermissible.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your stance, but I must insist that the consequences of our actions should be the primary concern. In this case, the potential to save millions of lives outweighs the moral rule against actively causing harm to one individual. The intention is to save lives, not to kill. The greater good must be considered, and in this scenario, the greater good is the preservation of millions of lives.

[Deontologist]: I see that we are at an impasse. While I respect your commitment to maximizing utility, I must uphold the deontological principles that guide my moral reasoning. The intention to kill, regardless of the potential benefits, is inherently wrong. I believe it is crucial to maintain the integrity of moral rules to preserve the inherent worth and dignity of every individual. I think we've explored this dilemma thoroughly, and I must now take my leave to reflect on our discussion. Farewell.
